bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Zum Poema Morale,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 1 (1878): 5–38.
bibliographic.title: Zum Poema Morale

entities.description: Zupitza's complete article introduces six witnesses, prints the Digby 4 version, compares their readings and order, constructs a stemma, rejects a proposed tenth- or eleventh-century exemplar, and dates the work about 1170.
entities.label: Julius Zupitza, Zum Poema Morale (1878)

rights.basis: The article was published in 1878; the Internet Archive item reports no visible copyright notice and identifies the item as NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: Zupitza's 1878 article, transcription, apparatus and stemma
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.accessMethod: No item-specific Digital Bodleian object or IIIF manifest was found in exact-shelfmark searches; use the catalog and imaging enquiry rather than assuming the manuscript is undigitized.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Thirteenth century; Zupitza more specifically proposed the beginning of the century

entities.description: The principal and directly transcribed witness. Zupitza places Poema Morale on the first fourteen leaves of a sixteen-leaf quire, ff. 97r–110v, in one ruled column of twenty-eight short lines per page; he assigns its hand to the early thirteenth century and its language to Kent. The current Bodleian catalog describes the whole codex as a thirteenth-century manuscript of 112 leaves.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Digby 4

notes: Poema Morale occupies ff. 97r–110v; the entire codex, not only these leaves, is the desired facsimile object.

rights.basis: The work and witness predate modern copyright by centuries; U-M also applies No Copyright–United States to the CME collection.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval textual content of Poema Morale and the Digby copy
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This does not determine rights in modern photographs, interfaces, editions or encodings.

entities.description: The earlier printed source known to Zupitza for thirty-seven selected Digby stanzas, beginning at the grammar's p. 222; it is an excerpt, not a complete Digby transcription.
entities.label: George Hickes, Linguarum veterum septentrionalium thesaurus, volume 1 (1705)

entities.description: A composite parchment miscellany containing two Poema Morale copies: E at ff. 7r–12v and the later-hand e at ff. 64r–70v. The first belongs to a thirteenth-century section copied by scribe 2; the second belongs to a first-half-of-the-thirteenth-century section copied by scribe 1 and lacks the final twenty-six lines.
entities.label: London, British Library Egerton MS 613

bibliographic.citation: Betty Hill, 'The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life, Formerly the Poema Morale or A Moral Ode,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 9 (1977): 97–144.
bibliographic.title: The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life, Formerly the Poema Morale or A Moral Ode

entities.description: A modern study of the title, seven complete witnesses, publication history, textual relationship, date, localization, metre and homiletic content; it cautions that each witness represents active textual reshaping.
entities.label: Betty Hill, The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life (1977)

next_steps: Email imaging@bodleian.ox.ac.uk with the exact shelfmark and request the complete current physical object: boards/covers and insides, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf and edges where offered.
Map Poema Morale separately to ff. 97r–110v and request an image inventory/canvas concordance.
Request separate written terms for private cache retention, public Git distribution, noncommercial publication, commercial print-on-demand and sublicensing.
openQuestions.question: Does the Bodleian already hold or permit creation of a complete color digital facsimile of MS. Digby 4, and what exact private-storage, research, Git-publication, print-on-demand and commercial permissions would govern it?

next_steps: Consult Zupitza's correspondence, working papers and later methodological statements.
Until direct physical use is evidenced, retain the printed editions as the immediate comparison layer and the manuscripts as their sources.
openQuestions.question: Did Zupitza inspect E, e, J, L or T directly, or did he construct all non-D comparisons solely from the cited Furnivall and Morris editions?

next_steps: Compare Hill's full textual arguments and later diplomatic editions witness by witness.
Treat Zupitza's U, Y, Z, X and W as historical editorial hypotheses, not material witnesses.
openQuestions.question: Which parts of Zupitza's 1878 U–Y/Z–X/W stemma remain accepted after Hill's revised analysis and later witness scholarship?

next_steps: Register through the official access route or ask Lambeth Palace Library for an inventory.
Request all physical surfaces, not only ff. 59v–65r, and keep the work mapping separate.
openQuestions.question: What do the two legacy Lambeth LUNA items for MS 487 contain, and is a complete whole-codex surrogate available after registration or by request?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile the manifest's 'Public Domain in most countries, other than the UK' wording with current systematic-extraction and publication terms.
Do not bulk-cache or redistribute the sequence until that scope is clear.
openQuestions.question: What permissions govern UK bulk retention and republication of the complete Egerton 613 IIIF image set?

next_steps: Preserve the source value in diplomatic output.
Add a non-destructive display annotation or normalization only if the publication workflow can distinguish source and corrected numbering.
openQuestions.question: Should downstream display silently correct the 1878 print's first stanza number 165 to 156, or preserve and annotate the source error?

reviewNotes: The medieval work, each manuscript witness, each nineteenth-century printed edition, the U-M encoding and the repository artifact are distinct layers.
Zupitza's main text is a Digby transcription. Ten T stanzas are passage-scoped note supplies at eight Digby lacunae, not a second continuous base.
Current Jesus 29 foliation is seventy-three lower than Zupitza's legacy foliation: current 169r–174v equals legacy 242r–247v.
The Trinity current catalog locates the poem at ff. 2r–9v / canvases 13–28. Hall's older 1r–8v numbering is not substituted here.
A complete public viewer is not automatically a downloadable or redistributable corpus asset. Egerton 613 remains linked but uncached under the conservative terms reading.
The cached Jesus and Trinity files cover the full provider-published source objects, while work_portion maps only the Poema Morale leaves. Completeness and target scope are intentionally separate.
No target-only manuscript excerpt is treated as a complete codex. Digby and Lambeth retain mediated whole-object request routes.
The complete Anglia scan is necessary to recover the actual stemma because the XML retains only a generic figure description.
Local caching and hard-link reuse do not grant permission to publish the files in Git or use noncommercially licensed images in commercial print-on-demand outputs.

lineage.summary: CME00086 is the repository copy of the complete University of Michigan electronic transcription of Julius Zupitza's 1878 article 'Zum Poema Morale,' Anglia 1: 5–38. The article prints the Digby 4 copy continuously on pp. 6–32, compares it with the five then-known other copy streams, proposes a stemma, and argues for composition around 1170. Zupitza says that his text follows Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Digby 4, ff. 97r–110v, apart from explicitly listed normalization and correction practices. His ten stanzas supplied from Trinity witness T occur only inside notes at eight Digby lacunae; they do not turn the main stream into an eclectic Trinity text. The comparison to E/e/J/L/T is routed through the earlier printed editions Zupitza names because direct consultation of those physical codices is not established. The exact 1878 volume and all named earlier editions are cached complete. No item-specific public Digby 4 facsimile was verified, so the record retains the official catalog and Bodleian whole-object imaging route. Complete public facsimiles of Jesus 29 and Trinity B.14.52 are cached as hard-linked whole-codex bundles with the Poema Morale portions mapped inside them; Egerton 613 has a complete 197-canvas viewer but is not bulk-cached under the British Library's geographically qualified terms, and Lambeth 487's legacy viewer remains access-gated. The XML preserves the complete printed page run but replaces Zupitza's graphical stemma with only a FIGDESC, losing the diagram's geometry.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Poema Morale: late twelfth century; Digby 4 copy: early thirteenth century; first complete Digby edition: 1878

catalogSummary.title: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)

catalogingSubject.label: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual subject is the anonymous late-twelfth-century Poema Morale as represented by Julius Zupitza's complete 1878 article and its principal Digby 4 transcription. CME00086 encodes the entire article, not only the verse: German introduction and witness census on pp. 5–6, the Digby text and apparatus on pp. 6–32, a German stemmatic study on pp. 32–36, and a German dating essay on pp. 36–38. The main verse stream is the Digby copy at ff. 97r–110v; ten stanzas from Trinity B.14.52 occur only as note-scoped supplies at eight Digby lacunae. The record keeps Zupitza's editorial prose, the other manuscript witnesses and prior printed editions, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML as distinct layers rather than assigning their dates, languages or responsibilities to the medieval work.

contentParts.label: Witness introduction and source census
notes: German is an edition-layer language and is not a whole-work language facet.
contentParts.summary: German introduction stating three aims and identifying D, E, e, J, L and T, their loci, selected dates, physical features and prior editions.

contentParts.label: I. Das Poema Morale nach D
notes: The medieval work itself is verse; this encoded part is marked mixed only because it substantively includes Zupitza's prose editorial declaration and notes.
The main Digby stream is 191 LG / 764 L. Ten T-supply LG / forty L are note descendants and are not counted as direct Digby lines.
The printed and encoded stanza after 155 is labeled 165, a source error for 156; the sequence then resumes 157 and later has the genuine 165.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous Digby 4 version of Poema Morale, printed as 191 numbered groups of four short lines with German editorial apparatus and ten Trinity-supplied stanzas embedded at eight lacunae.

contentParts.label: II. Verhältniss der Hss. des Poema Morale zu einander
notes: Four L elements encode a quoted two-line reading pair; they are apparatus quotation, not an intrinsic new poem section.
The printed p. 36 stemma is present only as FIGURE/FIGDESC in XML, so this part is structurally partial at the visual layer.
contentParts.summary: German textual analysis dividing the six witness streams into proposed groups, testing dependency through shared and divergent readings, and concluding with a graphical stemma.

contentParts.label: III. Alter des Poema Morale
notes: Zupitza's c. 1170 conclusion is a historical scholarly claim; modern work metadata retains Hill's broader late-twelfth-century analysis.
contentParts.summary: German dating essay rejecting Morris's proposed tenth- or early-eleventh-century source on lexical and rhyme grounds and proposing composition around 1170.

assertion.notes: This dates the abstract work, not the Digby copy. The normalized range follows Hill's historical proposal; Zupitza's c. 1170 falls within it.

dateStatements.display: Late twelfth century; Hill proposed 1154–1189, while Zupitza argued approximately 1170

assertion.notes: This is the first known physical attestation of the cataloged Digby version, not a claim that Digby is the earliest witness of Poema Morale across the tradition.

dateStatements.display: Early thirteenth century in Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Digby 4

assertion.notes: Hickes had printed thirty-seven selected Digby stanzas in 1705; 1878 is the first complete publication of this witness version, not the first publication of Poema Morale from any witness.

dateStatements.display: First complete Digby 4 transcription published by Julius Zupitza in 1878

extent.description: Encoding metrics for the complete article: one DIV1; four DIV2; 201 LG and 808 L in total. Section I contains 191 direct numbered Digby LG / 764 direct short-line L plus ten nested T-supply LG / forty L in notes. Section II adds four quoted L. These counts describe XML representation and apparatus, not the abstract work's DIMEV extent of 398 long lines in rhyming couplets. The source prints the Digby copy as 191 groups of four short lines, equivalent to 382 paired long lines if mechanically recombined.

formStatements.description: The cataloged medieval work is verse. CME00086 also retains extensive German editorial prose in three framing parts, but that prose belongs to Zupitza's edition rather than the anonymous work's form.

assertion.notes: Hill calls the work a verse-sermon; verse homily is the normalized corpus label.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German editorial prose is substantive in the encoded article but belongs to the edition layer and is therefore described at part level rather than exposed as a work-language facet.
Poema Morale is anonymous; Julius Zupitza is editor, direct Digby transcriber, collator and author of the German study, not author of the medieval work.
The source edition date 1878 is kept separate from the work's late-twelfth-century composition and the early-thirteenth-century Digby copy.
The Digby copy's Kentish classification is witness-level. It does not supersede Hill's distinct and tentative reconstruction of the original work's dialect region.

DIMEV's 398 lines describe the abstract work in its fullest long-line/couplet form. CME's 764 direct Digby L elements are short-line encoding units; ten T-supply groups and four quoted lines are apparatus.
The complete article is textually retained, but content_structure_status is partial because the p. 36 graphical stemma has been reduced to a generic figure description without its branch geometry.
German prose and Latin conventional title strings are recorded in titles and part descriptions but are not promoted to work-level language facets.
Whole-manuscript facsimiles for Jesus 29 and Trinity B.14.52 are supporting comparison sources, not the source of the continuous Digby text.
No public Digby 4 facsimile was inferred from the existence of a catalog or generic Digital Bodleian collection page.

next_steps: Review post-1977 metrical, dialectological and manuscript scholarship.
Keep original composition, witness language and copying place separate.
openQuestions.question: Can Poema Morale's composition be dated or localized more securely than late-twelfth-century composition and Hill's tentative southern South-East Midlands reconstruction around Middlesex or London?

next_steps: Compare the full readings in diplomatic editions and modern stemmatic studies.
Do not promote Zupitza's hypothetical nodes to physical witnesses or current consensus.
openQuestions.question: Which parts of Zupitza's 1878 U–Y/Z–X/W stemma remain accepted after Hill and later witness scholarship?

next_steps: Request all boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, leaves and edges where offered, not only ff. 97r–110v.
Map the work portion separately and obtain explicit Git, scholarly-publication and commercial print-on-demand terms.
openQuestions.question: Will Bodleian Imaging Services supply a complete color facsimile of the whole 112-leaf Digby 4 codex, and under what private-storage and republication terms?

next_steps: Preserve the source numbering in diplomatic output.
Add a transparent correction note only if the rendering model can distinguish source and normalized labels.
openQuestions.question: Should reader-facing output annotate the source's first stanza 165 as 156 while preserving the diplomatic value?

notes: This broad origin does not identify an exact composition site.

assertion.notes: Hill reconstructs the original version on the southern border of the South-East Midland dialect area around Middlesex or London; this is a critical localization, not documentary proof.

placeStatements.placeLabel: Southern South-East Midlands (Middlesex or London area)

assertion.notes: Zupitza assigns the scribe's linguistic forms to Kent. The claim belongs to this witness copy and is not transferred to the original work.

scope.description: Direct transcription of Digby 4, ff. 97r–110v, apart from explicitly stated editorial normalization and correction
Comparison of six witness streams through the cited Furnivall and Morris editions; direct physical consultation is established only for Digby 4
Authorship of the German witness census, stemmatic argument and dating essay

subjects.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor
Philology and textual criticism
notes: This describes substantive editorial-study parts retained by CME00086; it is not presented as a subject of the anonymous homily itself.

summaries.value: An aging speaker regrets a life wasted in sin and turns preacher, urging repentance, charity, almsgiving, detachment from transient wealth, and preparation for death and judgment. The poem contrasts the pains of hell with the unending joy of heaven and closes with a prayer to live and die well. CME00086 frames Zupitza's Digby 4 transcription with a German census of six witness streams, a detailed stemmatic argument, and a dating essay that rejects a tenth- or eleventh-century origin and proposes composition around 1170.

Four encoded sections retain the whole 1878 article: witness introduction (pp. 5–6); Digby text and apparatus (pp. 6–32); manuscript-relationship study and stemma (pp. 32–36); and dating essay (pp. 36–38). The Digby text contains 191 numbered four-short-line groups. Ten additional T groups at eight Digby lacunae are embedded in notes, and four verse lines are quoted in the stemmatic discussion.

The XML covers every printed page from 5 through 38 and retains Zupitza's prose, edited verse, notes, page breaks and Digby folio milestones. It does not encode an image or branch structure for the graphical stemma on p. 36, only the description 'family tree of manuscripts.' The full-volume scan is therefore required to inspect that diagram.

The main text is a witness-specific Digby version rather than a reconstructed critical text. Zupitza's T supplies are separately scoped note passages, and E/e/J/L/T remain comparison witnesses mediated by cited earlier print editions. The German article prose is described as editorial framing and is not promoted to the anonymous work's language or form facets.

assertion.notes: The qualifier distinguishes this complete article-level representation from the abstract work and from other witness versions; it is not a manuscript title.

titles.value: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)

scope.description: Printed title of Zupitza's article and inherited U-M item title

notes: This is the conventional scholarly title, not an authorial or manuscript title.

assertion.notes: Betty Hill proposed this descriptive title in 1977; Poema Morale remains the conventional DIMEV title.

titles.value: Ic am elder, þanne ic wes, a wintre and ec a lore
Crist ȝeue us lede swich lif and habbe swichne ende, þet we moten þider cumen, þanne we hennes wende

work.summary: An aging speaker regrets a life wasted in sin and turns preacher, urging repentance, charity, almsgiving, detachment from transient wealth, and preparation for death and judgment. The poem contrasts the pains of hell with the unending joy of heaven and closes with a prayer to live and die well. CME00086 frames Zupitza's Digby 4 transcription with a German census of six witness streams, a detailed stemmatic argument, and a dating essay that rejects a tenth- or eleventh-century origin and proposes composition around 1170.
